subject: Fwd: Yurt
body:
---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>
Date: Thu, May 15, 2025 at 3:47 PM
Subject: Re: Yurt
To: Don Gohore Be Huszar <antonio.hudnall@gmail.com>


Residential Window Supplier San Francisco | Premium Showroom
<https://truittandwhite.com/window-door-showroom>

On Thu, May 15, 2025 at 3:46 PM Don Gohore Be Huszar <
antonio.hudnall@gmail.com> wrote:

>
>    -
>
>       For a *10-foot-long* metal stud equivalent to a *2x4 wood stud*,
>       the most common gauge options are:
>       - *18-gauge (0.0478 inches thick)* – Strong and durable, often used
>          for *structural framing*.
>          - *20-gauge (0.0359 inches thick)* – Lighter but still sturdy,
>          suitable for *non-load-bearing walls*.
>          - *16-gauge (0.0598 inches thick)* – Heavier-duty, used for *high-load
>          applications*.
>
>       If your yurt requires *structural integrity*, *18-gauge or 16-gauge*
>       would be ideal. If the walls are *non-load-bearing*, *20-gauge*
>       could work while reducing weight and cost.
>
>       Would you like help sourcing materials or comparing costs?
>
>       That sounds solid! With *9 vertical beams per wall* and *8 wall
>       sections*, you’ll have *72 beams* in total. Keeping them *16 inches
>       apart* is a standard spacing for structural stability.
>
>       To calculate your *lumber needs*, you’ll want to consider:
>       - *Beam length:* Depending on your ceiling height, each beam will
>          likely be *9–10 feet long*.
>          - *Beam material:* If you go with *wood studs*, *2x4 or 2x6*
>          beams could work. For *metal*, gauge and framing type will
>          matter.
>          - *Fasteners & Connectors:* You’ll need *brackets, screws, or
>          welding points* for stability.
>
>       Would you like help estimating how much material you’ll need, or
>       advice on sourcing?
>
>       Great! You’ll need *top and bottom plates* to secure the wall studs
>       and provide structural integrity.
>       *Materials for Top & Bottom Plates:*
>          - *Wood Option:* *2x4 or 2x6* lumber (same as your studs).
>          - *Metal Option:* *Track channels* matching your *16-gauge metal
>          studs*.
>       *Quantity Needed:*
>          - Each wall section is *12 feet wide*.
>          - You have *8 sections*, meaning *8 top plates* and *8 bottom
>          plates*.
>          - Each plate should run across the *entire length of the wall*.
>          - *Total material length:* *8 × 12 feet = 96 feet* for top
>          plates and *96 feet* for bottom plates.
>          - If using *metal track*, ensure it’s rated for *structural
>          walls*.
>
>       Would you like recommendations on sourcing materials or
>       construction techniques for assembly?
>
>       Alright, let’s break it down!
>       *Material Costs:*
>          - You need *72 metal studs* at *10 feet* each.
>          - At *$1.51 per linear foot*, each stud costs *about $15.10*.
>          - *Total cost:* *72 studs × $15.10 = $1,087.20* (before tax and
>          shipping).
>       *Weight Calculation:*
>          - A *16-gauge metal stud* weighs *~50–60 lbs per 10-ft stud*.
>          - *Total weight for 72 studs:* *72 × 50–60 lbs = 3,600–4,320 lbs*
>          .
>          - Consider *transportation*—this is about *1.8–2.2 tons*.
>
>       Would you like help finding suppliers or alternative material
>       options to optimize cost and weight?
>
>       For *roof spines* and other structural materials, here are some
>       sourcing options:
>       - *ABC Supply Co., Inc.* – A major distributor of *roofing and
>          framing materials*, with a location in *San Rafael, CA*.
>          - *Roof Observations* – A resource detailing *roofing material
>          sources* used across the U.S., including metal and wood options.
>          - *ABC Supply* – A national supplier with *over 1,000 locations*,
>          offering *roofing, siding, and framing materials*.
>
>       Would you like help comparing materials for durability and cost?
>
>
> On Thu, May 15, 2025 at 11:19 AM Don Gohore Be Huszar <
> antonio.hudnall@gmail.com> wrote:
>
